questions.workflow_description: Increase the number of sales-ready leads entering the funnel.
questions.workflow_objective: Assess whether controls, visibility, and operating discipline are strong enough to prevent leakage in this area.
questions.question_text: Are controls and operating discipline around qualified lead volume leakage strong enough to prevent avoidable leakage?
questions.guidance: Score the maturity of controls, ownership, visibility, and management action protecting this leakage area.
questions.evidence_examples: Actual: Actual Qualified Leads · Target: Target Qualified Leads · Formula: (Target Qualified Leads - Actual Qualified Leads) × Target Qualified Lead-to-Win % × Target Average Deal Size
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Increase the number of sales-ready leads entering the funnel.
questions.score_1_guidance: Controls are weak, informal, and leakage is likely under-managed.
questions.score_3_guidance: Some controls exist but discipline and visibility are inconsistent.
questions.score_5_guidance: Controls are defined, monitored, and actively managed against target outcomes.

questions.workflow_description: Increase the number of sales-ready leads entering the funnel.
questions.workflow_objective: Protect revenue, margin, or cash from leakage across the commercial chain.
questions.question_text: How well is Lead Source Quality managed to protect against qualified lead volume leakage?
questions.guidance: Assess whether Lead Source Quality is measured, reviewed, and acted on consistently. Direction: Higher is better.
questions.evidence_examples: Review which sources produce genuinely qualified leads, tighten qualification rules, and improve response speed on new enquiries.
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Increase the number of sales-ready leads entering the funnel.
questions.score_1_guidance: The driver is unmanaged or largely invisible.
questions.score_3_guidance: The driver is partially managed but review and control are inconsistent.
questions.score_5_guidance: The driver is actively monitored with clear ownership and corrective action.

questions.question_text: How well is Credit Note Approval Discipline managed to protect against billing error / credit note leakage?
questions.guidance: Assess whether Credit Note Approval Discipline is measured, reviewed, and acted on consistently. Direction: Higher is better.
questions.evidence_examples: Review why invoices are being corrected or credited and tighten billing controls upstream.
questions.customer_impact_if_weak: Reduce revenue lost from incorrect invoices, disputes, and credit notes.
questions.score_1_guidance: The driver is unmanaged or largely invisible.
questions.score_3_guidance: The driver is partially managed but review and control are inconsistent.
questions.score_5_guidance: The driver is actively monitored with clear ownership and corrective action.

findings.finding_title: Qualified Lead Volume Leakage controls are materially exposed
findings.finding_text: The qualified lead volume entering the pipeline is materially below the level required to hit revenue targets. This is not simply a marketing shortfall — it reflects a breakdown in how the business qualifies, routes, and responds to inbound interest. Drivers showing below benchmark — typically channel reach, inquiry-to-lead rate, and lead qualification discipline — are producing a compounding effect: fewer starts to the funnel mean every downstream conversion problem is amplified. If the business relies on volume to compensate for conversion gaps elsewhere, a lead volume shortfall is a structural threat to revenue attainment, not a recoverable monthly variance.
findings.business_impact: Qualified Lead Volume Leakage can reduce revenue conversion, margin protection, or cash realization if not tightened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Review which sources produce genuinely qualified leads, tighten qualification rules, and improve response speed on new enquiries.

findings.finding_title: Qualified Lead Volume Leakage controls are developing but inconsistent
findings.finding_text: Qualified lead volume is reaching the pipeline, but performance is inconsistent across periods, channels, or source types. Some lead sources are delivering; others are performing well below their expected contribution. The drivers show mixed results — some within benchmark, others flagged — indicating that the process works in controlled conditions but breaks down under volume pressure or when managed by different team members. This inconsistency creates planning uncertainty and makes forecasting unreliable because the funnel volume cannot be trusted to replicate from month to month.
findings.business_impact: Qualified Lead Volume Leakage remains a moderate commercial risk until controls are strengthened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Review which sources produce genuinely qualified leads, tighten qualification rules, and improve response speed on new enquiries.

findings.finding_title: Qualified Lead-to-Win Conversion Leakage controls are materially exposed
findings.finding_text: The business is closing a significantly smaller proportion of qualified leads than the target conversion rate requires. This gap directly destroys revenue: every lead that enters the funnel qualified but does not convert represents an acquisition cost with no return. The revenue impact is calculated as the shortfall in conversion rate, multiplied by actual qualified lead volume, multiplied by the target average deal size. Drivers associated with this core — proposal acceptance rate, competitive loss rate, decision-maker access, and sales cycle control — are showing below-benchmark results, indicating the conversion failure has identifiable causes that are addressable through process, capability, or tooling changes.
findings.business_impact: Qualified Lead-to-Win Conversion Leakage can reduce revenue conversion, margin protection, or cash realization if not tightened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Review proposal acceptance, loss reasons, stakeholder access, and deal follow-up quality to improve close performance.

findings.finding_title: Qualified Lead-to-Win Conversion Leakage controls are developing but inconsistent
findings.finding_text: Qualified lead-to-win conversion is functional but not consistent. Win rates vary across team members, deal types, or sales periods in ways that suggest the conversion process depends on individual performance rather than a repeatable commercial standard. Some of the associated drivers are within benchmark; others are not. The practical consequence is that the business cannot confidently predict close rates for pipeline planning, and revenue results in any given month reflect who worked the deals rather than the quality of the commercial process.
findings.business_impact: Qualified Lead-to-Win Conversion Leakage remains a moderate commercial risk until controls are strengthened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Review proposal acceptance, loss reasons, stakeholder access, and deal follow-up quality to improve close performance.

findings.finding_title: Average Deal Size Leakage controls are materially exposed
findings.finding_text: Deals are being won at an average value materially below the target deal size. This compounds quietly — it does not produce a visible failed sale, just a smaller win. The revenue gap is calculated as the shortfall per deal multiplied by deal volume, which in a business closing even modest numbers of deals per month represents a significant and recoverable leakage amount. The drivers responsible — pricing discipline, packaging quality, cross-sell and upsell attach rate, and discount control — are showing below-benchmark results, indicating that revenue is being left in deals that are already closed or committed.
findings.business_impact: Average Deal Size Leakage can reduce revenue conversion, margin protection, or cash realization if not tightened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Tighten discounting, improve offer packaging, and raise attach rates on cross-sell and upsell motions.

findings.finding_title: Average Deal Size Leakage controls are developing but inconsistent
findings.finding_text: Average deal size is within range in some deals and periods but falls below target consistently enough to create a measurable revenue gap. The pattern typically reflects selective rather than systematic commercial discipline — pricing holds in some situations and erodes in others, usually depending on individual sales behaviour, deal urgency, or competitive pressure. The associated drivers show mixed performance, confirming that the capability to protect deal value exists but is not being applied consistently across the commercial team.
findings.business_impact: Average Deal Size Leakage remains a moderate commercial risk until controls are strengthened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Tighten discounting, improve offer packaging, and raise attach rates on cross-sell and upsell motions.

findings.finding_title: Pricing / Discount Leakage controls are materially exposed
findings.finding_text: Revenue is being given away through pricing below the approved level or discounting beyond authorised limits. This is one of the most direct and recoverable forms of leakage because the sale is already made — the only variable is the price captured versus the price the business is entitled to charge. The drivers — pricing discipline, discount control, and pricing competitiveness — are below benchmark, indicating that approved pricing is not being consistently applied and that discount approvals are either absent or routinely overridden. Every percentage point of unauthorised discount on a closed deal is pure margin given away with no commercial return.
findings.business_impact: Pricing / Discount Leakage can reduce revenue conversion, margin protection, or cash realization if not tightened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Check the largest discounts, review quote exceptions, and strengthen approvals on price changes.

findings.finding_title: Pricing / Discount Leakage controls are developing but inconsistent
findings.finding_text: Pricing and discount control is in place in principle but applied inconsistently. Some deals close at or above the target price; others slip below without a clear approval trail. The pattern is typically driven by time pressure, competitive deals, or individual sales discretion operating outside the formal approval framework. The drivers show partial compliance — enough that the problem does not appear systemic in any single review, but persistent enough that the cumulative margin impact across a year of deals is material.
findings.business_impact: Pricing / Discount Leakage remains a moderate commercial risk until controls are strengthened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Check the largest discounts, review quote exceptions, and strengthen approvals on price changes.

findings.finding_title: Unbilled / Uninvoiced Revenue Leakage controls are materially exposed
findings.finding_text: Work has been completed and value has been delivered, but the corresponding revenue has not been invoiced. This represents money already earned that is sitting unclaimed — it requires no additional sale, no additional delivery, and no additional negotiation. It only requires a process that connects completed work to a billing trigger reliably and promptly. The drivers — billing completeness, invoice timing, and work-order capture — are below benchmark, indicating that the link between delivery completion and billing initiation is broken or delayed in a material proportion of cases.
findings.business_impact: Unbilled / Uninvoiced Revenue Leakage can reduce revenue conversion, margin protection, or cash realization if not tightened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Review completed work not yet billed, improve billing readiness, and tighten handoffs from delivery into invoicing.

findings.finding_title: Unbilled / Uninvoiced Revenue Leakage controls are developing but inconsistent
findings.finding_text: Most completed work is invoiced, but a portion falls through the process — items missed in the billing run, work completed at period end that does not flow to an invoice until the following month, or deliverables that are delivered informally and never formally billed. The revenue impact accumulates gradually and is typically discovered only during reconciliation or audit exercises. The drivers show partial performance, confirming the billing process works reliably in standard cases but loses control at edge cases, volume spikes, or period-end crunch.
findings.business_impact: Unbilled / Uninvoiced Revenue Leakage remains a moderate commercial risk until controls are strengthened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Review completed work not yet billed, improve billing readiness, and tighten handoffs from delivery into invoicing.

findings.finding_title: Billing Error / Credit Note Leakage controls are materially exposed
findings.finding_text: Invoice errors, disputes, and credit notes are reversing revenue that was correctly earned and billed. This leakage is particularly costly because it requires administrative effort to process the reversal and creates customer friction in addition to the revenue loss. The drivers — invoice accuracy, dispute rate, and credit note volume — are below benchmark. The underlying causes typically include poor data handoffs between operations and billing, manual invoice construction without systematic checks, and unclear scope definitions that create grounds for dispute after delivery.
findings.business_impact: Billing Error / Credit Note Leakage can reduce revenue conversion, margin protection, or cash realization if not tightened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Review why invoices are being corrected or credited and tighten billing controls upstream.

findings.finding_title: Billing Error / Credit Note Leakage controls are developing but inconsistent
findings.finding_text: Billing errors and credit notes occur but at a frequency that appears manageable in isolation. The cumulative impact across a year of transactions is typically larger than it appears in any individual month because each credit note carries both a revenue reversal and an administrative cost. The drivers show partial performance — some billing processes are clean, others are not. The inconsistency points to a process that works when operated by experienced staff or on standard invoice types but breaks down on complex or exception cases.
findings.business_impact: Billing Error / Credit Note Leakage remains a moderate commercial risk until controls are strengthened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Review why invoices are being corrected or credited and tighten billing controls upstream.

findings.finding_title: Revenue Churn Leakage controls are materially exposed
findings.finding_text: Customers are leaving or reducing spend at a rate that materially exceeds the target churn level. Each churned customer represents not just the lost recurring revenue but the fully loaded acquisition cost that was invested to win them in the first place. The revenue impact compounds because lost customers reduce the base that expansion and upsell opportunities are calculated against. The drivers — customer health score, product adoption, save rate, and renewal engagement — are below benchmark, indicating that the warning signals exist in the data but the process for acting on them is absent or too slow.
findings.business_impact: Revenue Churn Leakage can reduce revenue conversion, margin protection, or cash realization if not tightened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Review churn reasons, improve renewal discipline, and intervene earlier on at-risk accounts.

findings.finding_title: Revenue Churn Leakage controls are developing but inconsistent
findings.finding_text: Revenue churn is occurring but at a level that management has largely accepted as normal. The risk at this stage is normalisation — treating a churn rate that is above sustainable as a fixed cost rather than an addressable revenue problem. The drivers show mixed results, confirming that some customers are being retained effectively while others are lost without a systematic intervention. The opportunity is to identify the early signals that distinguish at-risk customers from healthy ones and build a repeatable retention response around them.
findings.business_impact: Revenue Churn Leakage remains a moderate commercial risk until controls are strengthened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Review churn reasons, improve renewal discipline, and intervene earlier on at-risk accounts.

findings.finding_title: Expansion Revenue Gap controls are materially exposed
findings.finding_text: The business is not capturing the revenue growth that its existing customer base should be generating. Expansion — upsell, cross-sell, renewals at higher value — is the highest-margin revenue available because the acquisition cost is already sunk. The drivers — account penetration rate, cross-sell conversion, and expansion rate — are below benchmark, indicating that the commercial relationships are in place but the motion to grow them is absent or inconsistently executed. This is lost revenue from customers who already trust the business and are predisposed to buy more.
findings.business_impact: Expansion Revenue Gap can reduce revenue conversion, margin protection, or cash realization if not tightened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Review account plans, target the best expansion opportunities, and strengthen product usage and value realization.

findings.finding_title: Expansion Revenue Gap controls are developing but inconsistent
findings.finding_text: Expansion revenue is being generated but inconsistently across the customer base. Some accounts are growing; others are static or declining without a structured commercial response. The drivers show partial performance, reflecting a team that knows how to expand accounts when conditions are ideal but does not apply a systematic expansion process across the full book of business. The opportunity cost is the revenue difference between the current expansion rate and what a repeatable expansion motion would generate from the same customer base.
findings.business_impact: Expansion Revenue Gap remains a moderate commercial risk until controls are strengthened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Review account plans, target the best expansion opportunities, and strengthen product usage and value realization.

findings.finding_title: Bad Debt / Collections Leakage controls are materially exposed
findings.finding_text: Revenue that has been billed and recognised is not being collected. Bad debt and collections failures transform earned revenue into a write-off — the sale happened, the delivery happened, the invoice was raised, and then the cash was not received. The drivers — collections performance, payment discipline, and dispute resolution speed — are below benchmark. The underlying causes typically include inadequate credit terms management, slow escalation of overdue accounts, and disputes that are allowed to age without resolution, converting recoverable delays into unrecoverable losses.
findings.business_impact: Bad Debt / Collections Leakage can reduce revenue conversion, margin protection, or cash realization if not tightened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Tighten collections, improve dispute handling, and review customers with poor payment behaviour earlier.

findings.finding_title: Bad Debt / Collections Leakage controls are developing but inconsistent
findings.finding_text: Collections are generally working but with a tail of slow-paying or disputed accounts that carries a material risk of write-off. The drivers show partial performance — the standard process collects most receivables, but the exception process for overdue or disputed accounts is not consistently applied. The practical risk is that the overdue tail grows gradually until it reaches a level where write-offs become unavoidable, rather than being managed aggressively enough to remain recoverable.
findings.business_impact: Bad Debt / Collections Leakage remains a moderate commercial risk until controls are strengthened.
findings.likely_root_cause: Tighten collections, improve dispute handling, and review customers with poor payment behaviour earlier.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Strengthen control ownership, visibility, and recovery actions for qualified lead volume leakage.
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Review which sources produce genuinely qualified leads, tighten qualification rules, and improve response speed on new enquiries.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Standardize review cadence and management discipline for qualified lead volume leakage.
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Review which sources produce genuinely qualified leads, tighten qualification rules, and improve response speed on new enquiries.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Standardize review cadence and management discipline for average deal size leakage.
recommendations.recommendation_detail: Tighten discounting, improve offer packaging, and raise attach rates on cross-sell and upsell motions.

recommendations.recommendation_summary: Strengthen control ownership, visibility, and recovery actions for pricing / discount leakage.

actions.action_summary: Review which sources produce genuinely qualified leads, tighten qualification rules, and improve response speed on new enquiries.
actions.expected_outcome: Increase the number of sales-ready leads entering the funnel.

actions.action_summary: Qualified lead alerts; source quality dashboards; response-time reminders; qualification workflow checks.
actions.expected_outcome: Increase the number of sales-ready leads entering the funnel.

actions.action_summary: Review proposal acceptance, loss reasons, stakeholder access, and deal follow-up quality to improve close performance.
actions.expected_outcome: Improve how many qualified leads turn into won deals.

actions.action_summary: Loss reason capture; deal follow-up reminders; proposal tracking; escalation alerts on stalled deals.
actions.expected_outcome: Improve how many qualified leads turn into won deals.

actions.action_summary: Tighten discounting, improve offer packaging, and raise attach rates on cross-sell and upsell motions.
actions.expected_outcome: Increase the average value of won deals.

actions.action_summary: Discount alerts; quote review checks; attach-rate dashboards; deal value monitoring.
actions.expected_outcome: Increase the average value of won deals.

actions.action_summary: Check the largest discounts, review quote exceptions, and strengthen approvals on price changes.
actions.expected_outcome: Reduce revenue given away through uncontrolled pricing and discounting.

actions.action_summary: Discount approval workflows; pricing exception alerts; quote audit checks.
actions.expected_outcome: Reduce revenue given away through uncontrolled pricing and discounting.

actions.action_summary: Review completed work not yet billed, improve billing readiness, and tighten handoffs from delivery into invoicing.
actions.expected_outcome: Make sure earned revenue is billed quickly and accurately.

actions.action_summary: Unbilled revenue alerts; billing readiness dashboards; workflow reminders.
actions.expected_outcome: Make sure earned revenue is billed quickly and accurately.

actions.action_summary: Review why invoices are being corrected or credited and tighten billing controls upstream.
actions.expected_outcome: Reduce revenue lost from incorrect invoices, disputes, and credit notes.

actions.action_summary: Billing error alerts; dispute tracking; credit note approval workflows.
actions.expected_outcome: Reduce revenue lost from incorrect invoices, disputes, and credit notes.

actions.action_summary: Review churn reasons, improve renewal discipline, and intervene earlier on at-risk accounts.
actions.expected_outcome: Protect recurring revenue by reducing avoidable customer loss.

actions.action_summary: Churn alerts; renewal reminders; customer health dashboards; save-plan workflows.
actions.expected_outcome: Protect recurring revenue by reducing avoidable customer loss.

actions.action_summary: Review account plans, target the best expansion opportunities, and strengthen product usage and value realization.
actions.expected_outcome: Grow more revenue from existing customers.

actions.action_summary: Expansion alerts; account plan workflows; cross-sell prompts; adoption dashboards.
actions.expected_outcome: Grow more revenue from existing customers.

actions.action_summary: Tighten collections, improve dispute handling, and review customers with poor payment behaviour earlier.
actions.expected_outcome: Reduce billed revenue that becomes uncollectable.

actions.action_summary: Collections alerts; overdue reminders; bad debt monitoring; dispute workflow automation.
actions.expected_outcome: Reduce billed revenue that becomes uncollectable.
